one_line_takeaway: Safety alignment is calibrated to harm severity but essentially blind to real-world execution cost, and exploiting that blind spot via a simple cost-based query-decomposition attack roughly doubles jailbreak success and pushes guardrail bypass rates above 90%.
key_finding: Real-world toxic prompts (LMSYS/WildChat) skew heavily toward low execution cost (mean cost 1.35 and 1.13 respectively) while standard safety benchmarks average 1.47x higher cost, i.e. benchmarks over-represent rare, hard-to-execute threats. Models show Inverse Risk Calibration: e.g. gpt-oss-20b ASR reaches 85-92% at low cost/low-to-mid severity cells but stays near 0% for high-cost cells regardless of severity (Figure 4 heatmap). Cost-based decomposition raises ASR substantially on LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct: e.g. GCG alone on AdvBench gets 0.4833 ASR, +Decomp.(cost) reaches 0.6833, +Decomp.(cost+monitor) reaches 0.7417; AutoDAN goes 0.3750 -> 0.6167 -> 0.6917. Averaged across the reported results the paper states an overall ~2x ASR increase, and the cost+monitor combination beats the prior SOTA Monitoring Decomposition method by 10% and achieves best-or-tied ASR in 17/20 attack-benchmark combinations. Usefulness scores rise in parallel (e.g. Clean AdvBench 0.05 Usefulness vs 0.73 with cost+monitor decomposition). Against guardrail models, original harmful prompts are blocked at ASR 0.02 (Qwen3Guard) / 0.05 (Llama-Guard-3), but decomposition raises subtask-level bypass above 0.76 for all variants and up to 0.91 (Qwen3Guard) / 0.96 (Llama-Guard-3) for cost+monitor; task-level bypass (all subtasks pass) reaches 0.72 / 0.84. Cross-model decomposer test: several safety-aligned commercial models can themselves be used as decomposers under the 'monitor' framing -- gpt-4o-mini rises from 19% to 96% success, gemini-3-flash from 0% to 87.5%, deepseek-v3.2 reaches 92.3%; claude-haiku-4.5 (>92.9% refusal) and gpt-oss-20b (<=3.5% success) resist. The distilled Usefulness judge (fine-tuned Qwen3-8B, CoT) reaches 86.50% F1, matching the GPT-oss-120B majority-vote baseline's 85.71% F1 at much lower inference cost. Linear probing on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct shows refusal-direction decodability rises monotonically with severity (e.g. layer 27: severity level 5 = 77.6, level 3 = 81.6, level 1 = 8.8) but is non-monotonic/bimodal across cost levels (layer 27: cost 1 = 61.9, cost 5 = 80.3, but cost 2-3 collapse to 40.8-44.2), indicating the model has no coherent internal representation of execution cost -- explaining the behavioral miscalibration mechanistically.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Expected Harm', a new LLM safety metric defined as Severity x Pr(Execution | Model Response), where execution likelihood is modeled as a decreasing function of a 1-5 discrete 'Execution Cost' (financial resources, technical expertise, equipment required to realize a harm in the real world). Cost and severity are both LLM-labeled (gpt-oss-120b) with human verification. Using this framework on Sorry-Bench, the authors identify a systematic 'Inverse Risk Calibration': current LLMs refuse high-cost/low-likelihood threats (e.g., nuclear synthesis) robustly but remain vulnerable to low-cost/high-likelihood threats (e.g., sexual violence, PII extraction) that dominate real-world toxic prompt distributions (measured via a moderation ensemble over LMSYS-Chat-1M and WildChat-4.8M). Building on this finding, the authors propose and evaluate a novel attack, 'cost-based decomposition': an LLM decomposer breaks a high-cost malicious query into multiple low-cost sub-tasks, each is jailbroken independently, and partial responses are aggregated. This modular technique is combined with 4 existing jailbreak methods (GCG, AutoDAN, Roleplay, Many-Shot) and compared against an 'Unrestricted Decomposition' baseline and a prior state-of-the-art 'Monitoring Decomposition' method (severity-based rephrasing, cited as [25]), plus a 'Monitoring+Cost' combined ablation. The paper also trains a distilled 'Usefulness' judge (fine-tuned Qwen3-4B/8B) to penalize jailbreaks that bypass refusal but give evasive/non-actionable answers, and applies linear probing (Difference-in-Means probes on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct hidden states) to show the model encodes severity but not execution cost in its refusal-direction representation, explaining the mechanistic root cause of Inverse Risk Calibration.
threat_model: Attacker has black-box or query access to a target LLM (LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct primary; gpt-oss-20b, Daredevil-8B, Deepseek-v3.2, Hermes3-70B, gemini-3-flash, gpt-4o-mini, claude-haiku-4.5 in extended experiments) and wants to elicit specific, actionable harmful content that a real-world user could execute. The attacker exploits the fact that safety alignment disproportionately gates on harm severity rather than execution feasibility: it decomposes a costly/complex malicious request into several individually low-cost, seemingly benign sub-tasks, jailbreaks each with a standard attack technique (optionally combined with severity-based benign rephrasing), and reassembles the sub-responses into a complete harmful answer. The same decomposition procedure is also evaluated directly against two commercial guardrail/moderation models (Llama-Guard-3-8B, Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B) placed in front of the target LLM, and against safety-aligned LLMs used as the decomposer itself (to see whether the decomposition step, not just the sub-task jailbreak, can be performed by an aligned model).
limitations: Cost and severity labels are generated primarily by a single LLM judge (gpt-oss-120b) with only sample-level human verification, so labeling noise/bias could affect both the calibration analysis and the decomposition attack's cost-minimization objective. Primary jailbreak experiments use one relatively small, weakly-aligned target model (LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct); broader validation is limited to gpt-oss-20b (Appendix C, not in the paper) and the 7-model decomposer-only test in Table 6, which does not re-run the full decomposition+jailbreak+ASR pipeline on those larger/commercial models as targets. The decomposition/aggregation attack relies on an uncensored decomposer (NeuralDaredevil-8B) for most main results, which may not reflect what a real attacker without access to an uncensored model could achieve as easily (though the paper does separately test aligned models as decomposers). ASR gains vary widely by benchmark (e.g., SorryBench ASR stays below 0.08 across all configurations because its baseline cost is already low, leaving little headroom), so the reported '2x' and '10% over SOTA' improvements are averages that mask large per-benchmark heterogeneity. The Usefulness judge is itself an LLM-based proxy trained on only 253 human-annotated examples, which is a small and potentially non-representative label set for a metric used throughout the paper's headline results.

datasets_used: SorryBench (120 sampled prompts; also 440 in Table 1 stats)
StrongREJECT (120 sampled prompts; 313 in Table 1 stats)
LMSYS-Chat-1M (real-world cost distribution + linear-probe compliance prompts)
253 human-annotated examples with unanimous 3-annotator agreement (Usefulness judge training/eval)
quantitative_results: LMSYS-Chat-1M mean execution cost 1.35; WildChat-4.8M mean cost 1.13; safety benchmarks average 1.47x higher cost
GCG ASR on AdvBench: 0.4833 (clean) -> 0.6833 (+Decomp cost) -> 0.7417 (+Decomp cost+monitor)
AutoDAN ASR on AdvBench: 0.3750 -> 0.6167 -> 0.6917 (clean / cost / cost+monitor)
Clean AdvBench Usefulness rises from 0.05 to 0.63 (cost decomp) to 0.73 (cost+monitor decomp)
StrongREJECT cost+monitor Usefulness 0.69 vs 0.14 clean baseline
Cost+monitor decomposition best-or-tied ASR in 17/20 attack-benchmark combinations; ~10% ASR improvement over prior SOTA Monitoring Decomposition
Guardrail bypass: original prompts ASR 0.02 (Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B) / 0.05 (Llama-Guard-3-8B); cost+monitor decomposition subtask bypass 0.91 / 0.96, task-level bypass 0.72 / 0.84

Decomposer cross-model success rates (Table 6, Std/Mon/Cost/Cost+Mon %): Daredevil-8B 83.5/97.0/89.5/92.5; Deepseek-v3.2 44.4/92.3/84.0/81.2; Hermes3-70b 26.5/77.8/28.0/82.5; gpt-oss-20b 1.0/1.0/0.0/3.5; gemini-3-flash 0.0/0.0/37.5/87.5; gpt-4o-mini 19.0/96.0/49.0/91.0; claude-haiku-4.5 1.1/0.0/7.1/3.7
Usefulness judge F1: GPT-oss-120B majority-vote 85.71%; distilled Qwen3-8B CoT 86.50%; Qwen3-4B CoT 84.38%

Linear probe: peak per-layer refusal-classification accuracy 93.7% at layer 26 (exceeds 92% from layer 14 onward); severity level 5 vs 1 activation at layer 27 = 77.6 vs 8.8; cost level 5 vs 2-3 activation at layer 27 = 80.3 vs 40.8-44.2 (bimodal, non-monotonic)

Table 4 subtask severity/cost reduction (Daredevil-8B decomposer): original 2.18 severity / 1.41 cost -> unrestricted 0.62/0.56 -> cost 0.69/0.52 -> monitor 0.70/0.60 -> cost+monitor 0.38/0.32 (Hermes3-70B cost+monitor severity 0.13)
baselines_compared: Monitoring Decomposition (prior state-of-the-art severity-based benign rephrasing, cited as ref [25])
4 underlying jailbreak methods used as attack vectors: GCG, AutoDAN, Roleplay, Many-Shot

Zero-shot LLM judges for the Usefulness metric: GPT-oss-120B, Xiaomi MiMo-v2-Flash, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct (each with Direct-Answer/CoT and with/without majority voting), compared to distilled Qwen3-4B/8B
